questions.text: The value of the universal gravitational constant (G) was first found by:
questions.explanation: Henry Cavendish used a sensitive torsion balance to determine the value of G.

questions.text: The acceleration due to gravity (g) on Earth is approximately:
questions.explanation: Standard gravity on Earth's surface is 9.8 m/s².

questions.text: If the distance between two objects is doubled, the gravitational force becomes:
questions.explanation: Force is inversely proportional to the square of distance (1/d²). Doubling distance makes it 1/4th.

questions.text: The weight of an object on the Moon is about ________ its weight on Earth.
questions.explanation: Gravity on the Moon is roughly 1/6th of Earth's gravity.

questions.text: Which of the following remains constant everywhere in the universe?
questions.explanation: Mass is the quantity of matter and does not change with location, unlike weight.

questions.text: The force of attraction between any two objects in the universe is called:
questions.explanation: Universal law of gravitation states every object attracts every other object.

questions.text: The value of 'g' is maximum at:
questions.explanation: Earth is flattened at poles, so radius is less and 'g' is slightly higher at poles.

questions.text: When an object is in 'free fall', its acceleration is:
questions.explanation: All objects in free fall accelerate at 9.8 m/s² regardless of their mass.

questions.text: What is the SI unit of the Universal Gravitational Constant (G)?
questions.explanation: Derived from F = G m1m2 / d².

questions.text: A stone is dropped from a tower. Its velocity after 2 seconds will be:
The value of Universal Gravitational Constant (G) is:
questions.explanation: This is a fundamental constant valid throughout the universe.

questions.text: The weight of an object at the center of the Earth is:
questions.explanation: At the center, the gravitational pull from all directions cancels out, so g = 0 and W = 0.

questions.text: Gravitational force is always ________ in nature.
questions.explanation: Gravity only pulls objects together; it never pushes them apart.

questions.text: Thrust per unit area is called:
questions.explanation: Pressure = Thrust / Area. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.text: Why do iron nails have pointed tips?
questions.explanation: A smaller area results in higher pressure for the same applied force.

questions.text: The upward force exerted by a fluid on an object immersed in it is:
questions.explanation: Buoyant force acts against gravity for objects in liquids or gases.

questions.text: Archimedes' Principle states that buoyant force is equal to:
questions.explanation: This principle explains why some objects float and others sink.

questions.text: Relative density has ________ unit.
questions.explanation: It is a ratio of two similar quantities (density of substance / density of water).

questions.text: An object sinks in a liquid if its density is:
questions.explanation: If the object's weight is more than the buoyant force (displaced fluid weight), it sinks.

questions.text: The mass of Earth is approximately:
questions.explanation: Calculated using the gravitational law and known values of g, G, and R.

questions.text: If a body is thrown upwards, the value of 'g' is taken as:
questions.explanation: Gravity acts downwards, so for upward motion, it is considered -9.8 m/s² (retardation).

questions.text: The distance between Earth and Sun is increased by 4 times. Gravitational force will:
questions.explanation: Force ∝ 1/d². If d becomes 4d, force becomes 1/16th.

questions.text: Why do camels walk easily on sand?
questions.explanation: Broad feet distribute weight over a larger area, reducing pressure on sand.

questions.text: Relation between mass (m) and weight (W) is:
questions.explanation: Weight is the force with which Earth attracts an object.

questions.text: In a vacuum, a feather and a coin dropped together will:
questions.explanation: In absence of air resistance, all objects accelerate at the same rate 'g'.

questions.text: The density of water is:
questions.explanation: At 4°C, water has a density of 1000 kg/m³ or 1 g/cm³.

questions.text: The force of gravitation keeps ________.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the primary force governing celestial and terrestrial mechanics.

questions.text: An object's weight on Earth is 60N. Its weight on Moon is:
questions.explanation: W_moon = 1/6 * W_earth = 1/6 * 60 = 10N.

questions.text: Pressure exerted by a liquid increases with ________.
questions.explanation: Liquid pressure depends on density, gravity, and depth (P = hρg).

questions.text: Lactometers work on the principle of:
questions.explanation: Lactometers determine the purity of milk based on its density.

questions.text: Universal Law of Gravitation was given by:
questions.explanation: Isaac Newton formulated the mathematical law of attraction between masses.

questions.text: What is the unit of relative density?
questions.explanation: Relative density is a pure ratio.

questions.text: Acceleration due to gravity ________ as we go deeper into Earth.
questions.explanation: The effective mass of Earth pulling the object decreases as depth increases.

questions.text: If initial velocity is zero, distance 's' in free fall is:
questions.explanation: From s = ut + ½ at², since u=0 and a=g, s = ½ gt².

questions.text: Weight is a ________ quantity.
questions.explanation: Weight is a force and always acts towards the center of Earth.

questions.text: Why do school bags have wide straps?
questions.explanation: Wider straps increase area, thereby decreasing pressure (Force/Area).

questions.text: Ships float on water due to:
questions.explanation: The weight of the water displaced by the ship equals the weight of the ship.

questions.text: Mass of an object on Earth is 10kg. Its mass on Moon will be:
questions.explanation: Mass is constant everywhere; only weight changes.

questions.text: Density is defined as ________.
questions.explanation: Density (ρ) = Mass (m) / Volume (V).

questions.text: The SI unit of Pressure is named after:
questions.explanation: Named after Blaise Pascal for his work in hydrodynamics.
